problem.title: Unique Binary Search Trees
problem.description: Given an integer n, return the number of structurally unique BST's (binary search trees) which has exactly n nodes of unique values from 1 to n.
assumptions: Count structurally unique BSTs (not the actual trees).

example.explanation: 5 structurally unique BSTs with values 1..3. Catalan number: dp[n] = sum of dp[i-1]*dp[n-i] for i=1..n.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Dynamic Programming [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: dp[i] represents the number of unique BSTs with i nodes; when node j is chosen as root, the left subtree has j-1 nodes (dp[j-1] choices) and the right subtree has i-j nodes (dp[i-j] choices), so dp[i] is the sum of all such products. This follows the Catalan number recurrence and runs bottom-up so every subproblem is solved exactly once. The key insight is that structural uniqueness depends only on the size of each subtree, not the actual values placed in it.

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Catalan Number Formula [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: The answer is the nth Catalan number: C(n) = C(2n, n) / (n+1). Compute it using the multiplicative formula: C(n) = product of (n+k+1)/k for k in 1..n, all divided by (n+1). Equivalently use: C_n = (2n)! / ((n+1)! * n!). In practice compute iteratively: result = result * (n+k) / k for k=2..n, then divide by (n+1). O(n) time O(1) space.
